channel.title: Hello on Aahaan Singh
channel.description: Recent content in Hello on Aahaan Singh

item.title: Could YOU Be The Next Dictator Of The UChicago Student Body?
item.description: I think that you could, I really do! In fact, an attempt was made to this very end in the recent past, and while it failed, it carries important lessons for us, as potential leaders of the new government. We study history to inform our future actions, so let us examine the facts of this abortive abortion of the ancien régime and see how we can launch a more effective coup.

item.title: Kaleidoscope
item.description: I have a new article out in Firebird (that’s UChicago’s music magazine) about Siouxsie and the Banshees’s Kaleidoscope! This post that I wrote a while back was a collection of interesting tidbits that I found over the course of researching for this article. Enjoy!

item.title: 2Set 2nes on the 2be
item.description: My friend is not a fan of La Campanella. Despite being among Liszt’s best known works, he told me recently, it is not a particularly great piece of music, never mind the fact that the theme itself is Paganini’s. Back when I still played the piano (in elementary school), I would occasionally put on classical music before bed, usually Mozart or Mendelssohn or something. I can’t name a single thing Mendelssohn ever wrote, and I don’t think that I could back then either, but I still remember a few pieces from prehistoric era of music listening. La Campanella, which I discovered on a “hardest songs to play on the piano” listicle, was my favorite. Listening to it again for the first time in years, though, I realized that really isn’t very enjoyable to listen to. It’s an impressive piece from a technical standpoint and the theme is very catchy but it is also full of very abrupt tonal and textural switches that break its spell. The different passages have very little to do with each other and are embellished beyond the point of reason. I also expect that it’s not quite representative of Liszt because I don’t think anybody serious would (sidenote: That same friend recommended me the Transcendental Études, which I enjoyed, though I still find them to be too unstructured for my taste.) him otherwise.

item.title: Queen Margaret
item.description: The French Wars of Religion are an interesting period of history. My AP Euro class (way back when) focused far more on it than any other religious conflict of the era. I don’t think the 30 Years War even got a mention, which seems like a bit of an oversight. My interest in the period was revived recently so I went down a Wikipedia rabbithole, and found that many of the articles on the subject were incredibly comprehensive. The one on the 1559-1562 French Political Crisis is about 50,000 words, as are the two articles on the First French War of Religion combined. That’s not quite a book on the (sidenote: It's about a third the length of the book on the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth that I'm currently reading, so 130 pages. Also, I can't find recent statistics but I can be sure that the vast majority of Wikipedia articles are under 8,000 words.) but it is much more than one would expect to find on Wikipedia, whether because people don’t usually put so much effort into articles or because the point of an encyclopedia is to give brief overviews. Either way, I was interested enough in the subject to benefit from their length.

item.title: Demon Sheep
item.description:
Another edition of interesting things that I have encountered online.

Music

Adrian Janes Interviews Adrian Borland: To me, The Sound is the essential post-punk band. Jeopardy and From the Lions Mouth are passionate and steely works that hit you in the face with their sincerity. If there’s one thing you’ll read about The Sound, though, it’s that they’re underrated, which probably isn’t true now but was back when they played. Now, we have a wealth of insight into the workings of the band because Penny Black Music has released so many interviews of the people involved in it. Adrian Janes, who was in the band that would become The Sound, interviews lead singer/guitarist Adrian Borland in this multi-part interview and it’s really interesting to hear about the sort of struggles he had in leading a band that was too successful to be a side gig but not as successful as it deserved to be.

item.title: Graphic Design Is My Passion
item.description: Taking required art courses, as marginalized as they are, is a privilege of the university. The best class I have taken in college was a class offered by the creative writing department about making comics. The second best was a class I just took, also by the creative writing department, on graphic design. I suppose that the creative writing department is to the college what protists are to the tree of life.

item.title: Steiners
item.description: Stina Nordenstam is one of those strange figures that I am only aware of because of the internet. She’s a Swedish songwriter who was active in the ’90s and early 2000s, but she hasn’t released an album in two decades or left a trace online in one, and has therefore in one way ceased to exist. Regular readers (myself) will notice the appearance of the musical stalking tag on a lot of my music-related posts because I’m naturally disposed towards falling into rabbitholes, and in any case they’re half of what's interesting about music writing to me. With Nordenstam, though, I can’t really find anything after 2013. Supposedly (according to random forum posts), she had an accident working on her next album and just stopped releasing music. Whatever the case, her current albums remain.

item.title: Siouxsie Miscellanea
item.description:
For various reasons, I have been reading a lot about SatB recently. They’re a fascinating group of people and there’s a lot of interviews and whatnot that they’ve done. Some interesting tidbits from the recent readings:

The Johns

There were three Johns who played guitar for the Banshees: McKay, McGeoch, and (sidenote: John Valentine Carruthers, which is a great name that rivals my own. There's just something about it.) McKay is well-attested to: in addition to his excellent work on the first two albums, he and drummer Kenny Morris were the subject of “Drop Dead/Celebration,” the vicious diss track that opened the Kaleidoscope era. He also has an album of old recordings out this year, which are quite good. Previously, the only stuff he had out after SatB that I could find was a single as a member of Zor Gabor, “Tightrope,” and it was really good. This is more of the same style, jagged and muscular guitarwork complemented by sufficiently gothic sioux-alike vocals, though it doesn’t quite reach “Tightrope”’s pinnacle. Nevertheless, the first track is a ripper.

item.title: Skipping Stones
item.description: I went to Indiana Dunes recently, along with fifty other people, as the last among my friends to do so. The others went either last year or the year before, so I knew what to expect, but I somehow had trouble reconciling the concept of sand dunes with Indiana in the same way that I laughed at the billboards in Chicago advertising their beaches. It’s a case of taking what you can get, I suppose.

item.title: Havoc Steve
item.description:
I have gotten around to compiling the interesting things I’ve read over the past couple of weeks. Without further ado:

Music

  1. Siouxsie Sioux - A Teenage Obsession: People laugh at teenage girls for being obsessed with certain artists but it used to be a more common thing, and a more physical thing. This guy had some really interesting experiences with the Banshees in their early days until he fell out of love with them (also, arguably, in their early days). Getting an autograph from Siouxsie Sioux with an Iggy Pop lyric referencing a previous interaction you had with her sounds really cool. On a related note, Iggy’s “Sixteen” (from where said lyric comes) is incredible, possibly the closest he got solo to the Stooges’ stuff; I’m not sure I vibe with the lyrics, though.
  2. The Bland Ballad of Jesse Wells: I will admit that I had not heard of this guy before reading Steven Hyden’s piece on him, but it is a good piece that considers the meaning of “protest music”. Jesse Wells seems like the prime example of someone who dons the aesthetic of protest music without really sticking his neck out, so he’s keeping his cake and eating it too. Also, “he’s an Arkansas Muppet rather than a New York Doll” is an all-timer.
  3. Compilation Nation: Mutant Disco: Sydney Salk does some digging into a ZE Records comp, which, if you know anything about ZE Records, is necessarily interesting. Mutant Disco is more winking performance than music, I think, but there are some great songs to come from the label—my faves are Was (Not Was)’s “Tell Me That I’m Dreaming,” the disco version of James White’s “Contort Yourself,” and Cristina’s “Deb Behind Bars” (the alternate version; I have no clue what they were thinking with the original). Here’s a curiosity: there’s a comp called ZE Records Story 1979/2009 that’s entirely late ’70s and early ’80s stuff except for one song from a band called Michael Dracula. As far as I can tell, ZE Records went defunct in 1984, revived in 2003, released Michael Dracula’s (sidenote: It's pretty good—haunting in some places, angry in others, and interesting throughout. Just wish I had the lyrics somewhere.) in 2008 to a smidge of interest, and went defunct again some time later without releasing another new artist. The band’s singer now runs a record studio.

Art

  1. Pet Portraits for InkTober: Tina Koyama has a great blog on urban sketching in Seattle. I love the pet portraits that she’s done here—the eyes are so expressive and it feels like you’re getting some of the character of the pets in addition to their likenesses. In other words, the dogs are goofballs and the cat is a stoic creature.
  2. The Savage Sword of Susan: I was sad that John Allison’s miniseries ended. It felt a bit rushed, to be honest, but it was lots of fun.

Other

  1. Thank You For Being Annoying: The conception of things that drive people as the things in their control that annoy them is an interesting one. I definitely feel that way when I have something that I really want to do, because it distracts me from doing anything else. I don’t know if “good annoyance” is the only thing that drives people to do exceptional things but the argument for it being an important factor is a convincing one.
  2. Family Affair: I don’t follow tennis as much as I did before Nadal retired (and even then, I was mostly just following Nadal). That is unfortunate because I missed the unfolding of this incredible story of low-ranked cousins making the finals of the same masters tournament. What are the chances?

Evidently, an interesting few months. Here’s the previous library post for a refresher on what happened before then!

item.title: Immaculate Grid
item.description: Can you solve this Immaculate (Music) Grid?

item.title: What Are Music Critics For?
item.description:
I’ve talked to a lot of people about where they find new music. One gets them from talking to fellow musicians. Another solicits them from friends. Many are dependent on Spotify’s suggestions to some degree. None of them actually read musical criticism in judging what to listen to.

Why is that? More importantly, does it matter?

Friction

Music has historically been on a trajectory towards greater accesibility. For a long time, it could only be heard live. Then, vinyl records brought music to the home, on demand. The CDs that superseded them were even more portable and durable, and files eliminated the need for a physical medium altogether.

item.title: Music, To My Ears
item.description:
I

The mayoral primary was heating up when I arrived in New York City on a humid June morning. By the time I’d reached the apartment, three flights up, I’d absorbed the spirit: an hour of lugging my bags down, up, and around had made me sweaty, and the twenty minutes I waited for someone to open the door had caused it to crust. The floor had tracks of New York dirt, the counters were dusted with New York crumbs, and the bathroom had that special New York scent—all for a New York rent. I really was in New York.

item.title: Call And Response
item.description: I feel like there has been a mismatch between the amount that I’ve read over the summer and what I have to “show” for it. Generally, when somebody talks about what they’ve read (or wants to impress on somebody just how much they’ve read), they will be referring exclusively to books. The book as a structure seems to carry with it an inherent prestige, which is warranted to a degree because being immersed in an author’s universe, whether fictional or some component of the real, requires many words. It’s a similar phenomenon to how RYM incentivizes people to listen to whole albums rather than random songs. This is a good thing, but books are not the exclusive stores of literary or intellectual merit.

item.title: Library
item.description:
In my first year of college humanities, my class was instructed to keep a commonplace book of quotes and comments for each of our assigned readings to get us thinking critically about them. This had the effect of making the unit more interesting, but also of making the idea of keeping a commonplace book less attractive. Still, the idea held some appeal to me.

Over the past couple of years, I have been looking for interesting blogs and websites to follow. Everybody does this whether they’re thinking about it or not, though much of the work is done by the algorithms that we are subject to. I wanted to remove this element, because, in addition to the well-documented risks and harms of passively being fed information, it just doesn’t get me anywhere interesting. It’s not very common anymore to bump into something surprising or exciting on one’s daily trundles through cyberspace. Doing so requires a bit more intentionality—leaping through hyperlinks, I suppose.

item.title: A Website
item.description:
I decided a few weeks back, while interning at Tudor, to create a new website.

The initial reason was that circumstances conspired to make me use a command line editor in order to debug something. In the second semester of my freshman year, I was forced to use Emacs for a week. No reason was given. I did not enjoy this task from above because Emacs felt cumbersome and the commands they gave us were stuff like “move your cursor to the next instance of a character” and “select and copy text”. It makes sense to teach basic commands to someone starting out on an editor, but I was never given a reason why I should use Emacs at all. Emacs is Emacs because of its huge and, most importantly, extensible1 library of modes and commands. Anyways, I still barely know any Emacs and haven’t used it for any big projects yet but I have been using Orgmode semi-regularly and have found it quite useful.
